skill.name: github
skill.description: GitHub CLI command guide for managing repositories, issues, pull requests, workflows, releases, secrets, keys, and GitHub API requests.
skill.summary: Use GitHub CLI commands to manage common repository and collaboration workflows.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command-execution findings, but these are primarily Markdown command examples for the GitHub CLI rather than embedded Ruby or shell execution by the skill itself. The skill is legitimate but medium risk because it instructs authenticated operations that can mutate repositories, manage workflows, publish secrets, register SSH keys, install extensions, and merge or delete GitHub resources.

medium_findings.title: Privileged GitHub CLI Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents authenticated GitHub CLI commands that can create repositories, merge pull requests, disable workflows, run deployments, publish releases, delete releases, and change project state. These commands are legitimate for a GitHub operations skill, but they can cause account or repository impact if an agent runs them without explicit user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explicit GitHub CLI examples for state-changing repository, pull request, workflow, and release operations. The commands are normal for this skill, but their impact is clear and direct.

medium_findings.title: Secrets and Key Management Commands
medium_findings.description: The skill includes commands for adding an SSH public key and managing GitHub Actions secrets. These examples are expected for GitHub administration, but they touch credential-adjacent workflows and require careful user authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly cover SSH key registration and secret set/delete operations. The SSH key path is a public key, not a private key, so the concern is operational misuse rather than confirmed credential theft.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party GitHub Extension Installation
medium_findings.description: The skill shows how to install a GitHub CLI extension by owner and repository. This can be useful, but it introduces supply-chain risk if an agent installs untrusted extensions without source review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The extension installation command is explicit and would fetch code from a third-party repository. The placeholder owner/repository means no specific malicious extension is identified.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown Fence Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer labeled many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is documentation containing fenced GitHub CLI examples, not executable Ruby code embedded in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown code fences and prose examples. No Ruby code path or automatic command runner is present in the reviewed file.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Detections Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns at cited lines, but the lines contain the skill description, example issue text, request text, or a color value. No evidence found of cryptographic code or algorithm selection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines found no crypto APIs, hashes, ciphers, or security algorithms. The matches appear to be substring or token collisions.

low_findings.title: Static SSH Private Key Detection Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The analyzer treated the SSH key example as private key access, but the cited command references a public key file. Public key registration still deserves user approval, but this is not private key exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited path ends with a public key suffix and is used with a GitHub CLI add-key command. No private key read or network exfiltration pattern was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated CLI Session Management
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes GitHub CLI authentication and token refresh commands. These are legitimate but should be run only after confirming the target account and requested scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly cover login status, login, and refresh flows. They are not malicious, but they affect authenticated account state.

dangerous_patterns.title: State-Changing Repository and Workflow Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists commands for creating repositories, merging pull requests, controlling workflows, and deleting releases. These operations can change production workflows or published artifacts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit state-changing GitHub operations. The risk depends on whether an agent executes them with user consent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Adjacent Secret and Key Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents SSH key addition and GitHub Actions secret management. These commands can affect account access and deployment credentials.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly mention SSH keys and secrets. No private key theft is shown, but the administrative scope is sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Extension Installation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes installing GitHub CLI extensions from another repository. Extensions may execute code locally, so untrusted sources require review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The installation example is generic and not tied to a malicious repository. The supply-chain risk is real but contextual.

content.value_statement: GitHub work often spans repositories, issues, pull requests, releases, workflows, and secrets. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical GitHub CLI reference for common operational tasks.
actual_capabilities: Guide GitHub CLI authentication checks, login, and token refresh workflows.
Provide commands for cloning, creating, forking, viewing, and listing repositories.
Support issue and pull request workflows, including comments, labels, reviews, checks, and merges.
Show GitHub Actions commands for listing, viewing, watching, rerunning, enabling, and disabling workflows.
Cover releases, gists, labels, projects, GitHub API calls, secrets, variables, aliases, and configuration.
limitations: It is a command reference, not an automatic GitHub policy or permission checker.
It assumes the GitHub CLI is installed and authenticated in the user environment.
It does not validate repository names, branch state, or organization rules before commands run.
Dangerous actions such as merges, deletes, secret changes, and workflow changes require explicit user approval.

use_cases.title: Open and Review Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Find pull requests, inspect their checks and diffs, leave reviews, and merge approved changes with the GitHub CLI.

use_cases.title: Operate CI and Releases
use_cases.description: Inspect workflow runs, rerun failed jobs, trigger manual workflows, and manage release artifacts from a terminal.

use_cases.title: Manage Repository Collaboration
use_cases.description: Create issues, add labels, assign owners, manage gists, and browse repository data without leaving the command line.

prompt_templates.title: Check My GitHub Status
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner authentication and repository context check.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to help me check my GitHub CLI authentication status and identify the current repository context.

prompt_templates.title: Create an Issue
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic issue creation with clear title and body.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to prepare a GitHub CLI command that creates an issue titled "Bug: Login fails" with a concise body. Ask before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Pull Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate pull request inspection and review workflow.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to guide me through checking pull request 45, viewing its diff and checks, and preparing a review response.

prompt_templates.title: Operate a Release Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced release and workflow operation with approval gates.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to plan GitHub CLI steps for checking workflow runs, rerunning failed jobs, creating a release, and uploading an asset. Do not run state-changing commands until I approve each one.

output_examples.input: I need to see whether pull request 45 is ready to merge.
output_examples.output: The assistant suggests checking the pull request details, viewing the diff, reviewing CI checks, and confirming approval status before proposing a merge command.

output_examples.input: Create a release for version 1.0 with generated notes.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares the release workflow, confirms the target tag and repository, and asks for approval before running the release creation command.

output_examples.input: Add a production secret for GitHub Actions.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains the secret update flow, warns that secret values should not be exposed in chat, and recommends interactive entry or a trusted local file.

best_practices: Confirm the repository, branch, and account before running any state-changing command.
Use read-only commands first to inspect pull requests, workflow runs, releases, and secrets.
Require explicit user approval before merges, deletes, workflow changes, secret changes, or extension installs.
anti_patterns: Running merge, delete, or workflow commands without confirming the target repository and branch.
Pasting secret values into chat instead of using interactive entry or trusted local files.
Installing GitHub CLI extensions from unreviewed repositories.

faq.question: Does this skill run GitHub commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a reference for GitHub CLI commands. The agent still needs tool access and user approval to execute commands.

faq.question: What tools must be installed?
faq.answer: The GitHub CLI must be installed. Git is also needed for branch, commit, push, and pull request workflows.

faq.question: Can it manage GitHub Actions secrets?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes commands for listing, setting, and deleting secrets, but those actions should always require explicit approval.

faq.question: Can it create and merge pull requests?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers creating, viewing, reviewing, checking, closing, and merging pull requests through the GitHub CLI.

faq.question: Is this safe for organization repositories?
faq.answer: It can be safe when permissions are scoped and commands are reviewed. Organization rules and branch protections still apply.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is legitimate, but it documents commands that can change repositories, workflows, secrets, releases, and account keys.
